description: Phase 2: Dependency analysis, topological sort, per-object conversion instructions

conversion_levels.description: Standalone function — no dependencies on other discovered objects
conversion_levels.strategy: Parallel conversion — convert before any procedure that calls it

conversion_levels.description: Leaf procedures — depend only on external tables, not on other discovered procedures/functions
conversion_levels.strategy: Parallel conversion — both have zero intra-object dependencies; convert after Level 0

conversion_levels.description: Orchestrator and audit procedures — call Level 0 function and/or Level 1 logger
conversion_levels.strategy: Sequential within level: audit first (Moderate), main pipeline last (Complex); both need Level 0+1 signatures before starting

dependency_edges.relationship: calls via table-valued function reference in FROM clause (line 166)

conversion_instructions: Line 47: Function signature → `def ufn_split_csv_int(csv_str: str | None) -> list[int]` — return a plain Python list since the only caller (line 166) uses it to build a filter set, not a joined DataFrame
Line 48: `RETURNS @t TABLE (Item INT NOT NULL)` → return `list[int]`; no DDL, no DataFrame needed at this level
Line 51: `COALESCE(@csv, N'')` → `csv_str = csv_str or ''`
Line 53: `REPLACE(REPLACE(@x, CHAR(10), ''), CHAR(13), '')) + ','` → `import re; csv_str = re.sub(r'[\r\n]', '', csv_str)` then split on ','

Lines 54-62: Entire WHILE loop (CHARINDEX / SUBSTRING / LTRIM/RTRIM / TRY_CONVERT) → replace wholesale with: `tokens = [t.strip() for t in csv_str.split(',')]; result = [int(t) for t in tokens if t.strip().lstrip('-').isdigit()]` — one-liner eliminates all manual string navigation
Line 59: `TRY_CONVERT(INT, @token) IS NOT NULL` → wrap `int(t)` in try/except ValueError; discard non-numeric tokens silently (matches TRY_CONVERT NULL-on-failure semantics)
Line 60: `INSERT @t (Item) VALUES (CONVERT(INT, @token))` → `result.append(int(t))` — list append replaces table insert
Line 63: `RETURN` → `return result`

Caller usage (line 166 of main pipeline): `customer_ids = ufn_split_csv_int(params.customer_ids_csv)` then use `F.col('CustomerID').isin(customer_ids)` for the semi-join filter — no DataFrame creation needed
limitations_found: WHILE loop with manual string parsing eliminated — no TODO needed, full rewrite as Python list comprehension is straightforward
TABLE variable @t eliminated — return Python list directly

usp_AW19_Log.dependency_signatures: none — depends only on external table dbo.AW19_RunLog
conversion_instructions: Lines 28-34: Procedure signature → `@dataclass class RunLogEntry` with fields run_id: str, proc_name: str, step_name: str, step_status: str, message: str | None = None, complete: bool = False
Line 37: `SET NOCOUNT ON` → skip entirely

Lines 38-40: `INSERT dbo.AW19_RunLog (...)` → write a single-row DataFrame to a Delta Lake table `dbo.AW19_RunLog` using `spark.createDataFrame([row_dict], schema).write.format('delta').mode('append').saveAsTable('dbo.AW19_RunLog')`
Line 38: `RunId, ProcName, StepName, StepStatus, Message` → map directly to dataclass fields
Line 39: `SYSUTCDATETIME()` for StartedUtc → `datetime.now(timezone.utc)` — use Python stdlib, not F.current_timestamp(), since this runs in driver context
Line 40: `CASE WHEN @Complete = 1 THEN SYSUTCDATETIME() ELSE NULL END` → `datetime.now(timezone.utc) if complete else None` — Python ternary replaces CASE
Line 34: `@Complete BIT = 0` → `complete: bool = False` — BIT to Python bool (default False)
Line 29: `@RunId UNIQUEIDENTIFIER` → `run_id: str` — pass UUID string from caller; generate with `str(uuid.uuid4())` at pipeline start (line 147 of main proc)
Output function signature: `def log_step(spark: SparkSession, run_id: str, proc_name: str, step_name: str, step_status: str, message: str | None = None, complete: bool = False) -> None`
Consider wrapping in a `RunLogger` class (instance holds spark, run_id, proc_name) to avoid repeating those args on every call — 12 call sites in main pipeline
gotchas_relevant: #10 BIT NULL handling — @Complete BIT has default 0 (not NULL), so safe to map directly to Python bool; but document that if NULL were passed, it would need `complete = bool(complete) if complete is not None else False`
limitations_found: dbo.AW19_RunLog table DDL (lines 10-22) must be pre-created as a Delta table before this function runs — add a `_ensure_log_table(spark)` setup function with CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS
SYSNAME type for @ProcName → use plain str; no length constraint enforced in PySpark

usp_AW19_ResolveTierOverride.dependency_signatures: none — depends only on external Person schema tables; NOT called by any discovered object
conversion_instructions: Lines 608-611: Procedure signature → `def resolve_tier_override(spark: SparkSession, customer_id: int, current_tier: str) -> str` — OUTPUT parameter @ResolvedTier becomes the Python return value
Line 614: `SET NOCOUNT ON` → skip
Lines 616-619: `DECLARE @PhoneCount INT` etc. → plain Python variables: `phone_count: int`, `email_count: int`, `person_type: str | None`, `title: str | None`

Lines 621-623: Scalar variable assignment via SELECT from Person.Person → `row = spark.table('Person.Person').where(F.col('BusinessEntityID') == customer_id).select('PersonType', 'Title').first()` then `person_type = row['PersonType'] if row else None; title = row['Title'] if row else None`

Line 625: `SELECT @PhoneCount = COUNT(*) FROM Person.PersonPhone WHERE BusinessEntityID = @CustomerID` → `phone_count = spark.table('Person.PersonPhone').where(F.col('BusinessEntityID') == customer_id).count()`

Line 626: `SELECT @EmailCount = COUNT(*) FROM Person.EmailAddress WHERE BusinessEntityID = @CustomerID` → `email_count = spark.table('Person.EmailAddress').where(F.col('BusinessEntityID') == customer_id).count()`
Lines 629-636: CASE block on scalar variables → Python if/elif/else chain (not F.when — all values are Python scalars, not column expressions)
Line 631: `@PersonType = 'SC'` string comparison relies on CI_AS collation → `(person_type or '').upper() == 'SC'` to normalize case
Line 632: `@Title IN ('Mr.','Ms.','Mrs.','Dr.')` → `(title or '').strip() in {'Mr.', 'Ms.', 'Mrs.', 'Dr.'}` — exact match safe here since titles are standardized, but document assumption
Line 630: `@CurrentTier = 'Standard'` etc. → direct Python string equality; confirm upstream values are title-cased consistently (gotcha #2 risk if source data has mixed case)

Lines 625-626: Three separate Spark table reads per customer call — this function is designed for scalar/per-customer use; if called in bulk (batch), refactor to accept a pre-joined DataFrame instead of per-row lookups
Return statement: `return resolved_tier` replacing `SET @ResolvedTier = CASE...END`
gotchas_relevant: #2 Case-sensitive string comparison — lines 631-632 compare PersonType and Title against hardcoded literals; SQL Server CI_AS would match 'sc'='SC', Python would not; use `.upper()` or `.strip()` normalization
#2 Case-sensitive string comparison — line 630 compares @CurrentTier to 'Standard'/'Bronze'/'Silver'/'Gold'; ensure upstream tier assignment always produces title-cased strings
limitations_found: OUTPUT parameter @ResolvedTier (line 611) → return value; call sites must be updated from `EXEC ... @ResolvedTier OUTPUT` to `resolved = resolve_tier_override(spark, cid, tier)`
Scalar SELECT from .first() (lines 621-623) — if BusinessEntityID not found, row is None; add null guard: `if row is None: return current_tier`
# TODO: Per-row Spark table reads (lines 625-626) are efficient only for small lookup volumes; if called in a loop over many customers, rewrite as vectorized join on a customer DataFrame

usp_AW19_AuditPricingViolations.dependency_signatures: RunLogger class with method `log_step(step_name, status, message, complete=False) -> None`; OR standalone `def log_step(spark, run_id, proc_name, step_name, step_status, message=None, complete=False) -> None`
conversion_instructions: Lines 643-647: Procedure signature → `def audit_pricing_violations(spark: SparkSession, run_id: str, start_date: date, end_date: date) -> tuple[DataFrame, int]` — @ViolationCount OUTPUT becomes second tuple element; violations DataFrame is first
Line 650: `SET NOCOUNT ON` → skip
Lines 652-654: `DECLARE @ProcName`, `@AuditStatus`, `@AuditMessage` → Python variables; proc_name as module-level constant `PROC_NAME = 'usp_AW19_AuditPricingViolations'`
Line 656: `IF OBJECT_ID('tempdb..#Violations') IS NOT NULL DROP TABLE #Violations` → skip entirely; PySpark DataFrame lifecycle is auto-managed

Lines 684-688: Build base joins — `detail = spark.table('Sales.SalesOrderDetail'); header = spark.table('Sales.SalesOrderHeader'); territory = spark.table('Sales.SalesTerritory'); product = spark.table('Production.Product')` then chain `.join()` calls with explicit `how=` parameter
Lines 685-686: `JOIN SalesOrderHeader ON SalesOrderID` → `detail.join(header, on='SalesOrderID', how='inner')`
Line 686: `JOIN SalesTerritory ON TerritoryID` → `.join(territory, on='TerritoryID', how='inner')`
Line 687: `JOIN Product ON ProductID` → `.join(product, on='ProductID', how='inner')`
Lines 688-689: LEFT JOINs to ProductSubcategory and ProductCategory → chain `.join(..., how='left')`

Lines 690-694: LEFT JOIN to AW19_PricingRules with date-range overlap condition → `pricing_rules = spark.table('dbo.AW19_PricingRules'); join_cond = (F.col('pr.CategoryName') == F.col('pc.Name')) & (F.col('pr.TerritoryGroup') == F.col('st.Group')) & (F.col('pr.EffectiveDate') <= F.col('h_order_date')) & (F.col('pr.ExpiryDate').isNull() | (F.col('pr.ExpiryDate') >= F.col('h_order_date')))` — the ExpiryDate IS NULL condition is critical for NULL handling

Line 693: `pr.EffectiveDate <= CAST(h.OrderDate AS DATE)` and line 694: `(pr.ExpiryDate IS NULL OR pr.ExpiryDate >= CAST(h.OrderDate AS DATE))` → must use `F.col('ExpiryDate').isNull() | (F.col('ExpiryDate') >= order_date_col)` — SQL OR with NULL on left side is NOT automatically handled in PySpark the same way as SQL WHERE

Line 695: `WHERE CAST(h.OrderDate AS DATE) BETWEEN @StartDate AND @EndDate` → `.where(F.col('OrderDate').cast('date').between(F.lit(start_date), F.lit(end_date)))` — cast start_date/end_date as `F.lit()` with DateType

Lines 696-702: Multi-predicate WHERE with OR (violation conditions) → `.where((F.col('UnitPriceDiscount') > F.coalesce(F.col('DiscountPct'), F.lit(0)) + F.lit(0.02)) | (F.col('UnitPriceDiscount') < 0) | (F.col('UnitPrice') <= 0) | (F.col('LineTotal') < 0) | (F.col('OrderQty') <= 0))` — each condition wrapped in parentheses, joined with `|`
Lines 664-671: ViolationType CASE block → chained `F.when(...).when(...).otherwise('OK')` — convert in same order as SQL CASE (order matters for overlapping conditions)
Lines 672-679: Severity CASE block → same `F.when` chain producing integer values 0/2/3
Line 663: `COALESCE(pr.DiscountPct, 0)` → `F.coalesce(F.col('DiscountPct'), F.lit(0))` — note: 0 must be cast to DecimalType(5,4) to match column type: `F.lit(Decimal('0')).cast(DecimalType(5,4))`
Line 682: `CAST(h.OrderDate AS DATE) AS OrderDate` → `F.col('OrderDate').cast('date').alias('OrderDate')`
Line 683: `INTO #Violations` → `violations_df = (...).cache(); violations_df.count()  # force materialization`

Line 704: `SELECT @ViolationCount = COUNT(*) FROM #Violations` → `violation_count = violations_df.count()` — BUT since .count() was already called for cache materialization, store that result: `violation_count = violations_df.count()` (single call OK since cached)
Lines 706-707: `@AuditStatus` and `@AuditMessage` Python variables → `audit_status = 'WARN' if violation_count > 0 else 'OK'; audit_message = f'Violations found: {violation_count}'`
Lines 709-714: `EXEC dbo.usp_AW19_Log ...` → `log_step(spark, run_id, PROC_NAME, 'AUDIT_VIOLATIONS', audit_status, audit_message, complete=True)`
Lines 716-721: Final SELECT ORDER BY Severity DESC, OrderDate DESC → `return violations_df.orderBy(F.col('Severity').desc_nulls_last(), F.col('OrderDate').desc_nulls_last()), violation_count`
gotchas_relevant: #3 NULL ordering in ORDER BY — line 721 `ORDER BY Severity DESC, OrderDate DESC`; SQL Server: NULLs last in DESC. PySpark: NULLs FIRST in DESC. Use `.desc_nulls_last()` on both columns

#2 Case-sensitive string comparison — ViolationType string literals ('OVER_DISCOUNT', 'OK', etc.) are generated here, not compared; no CI risk. However CategoryName comparisons if used downstream must use F.lower()

#5 Decimal precision loss — line 697 `d.UnitPriceDiscount > COALESCE(pr.DiscountPct, 0) + 0.02` mixes DECIMAL(5,4) with float literal; cast 0.02 as `F.lit(Decimal('0.02')).cast(DecimalType(5,4))` and set `spark.sql.decimalOperations.allowPrecisionLoss=false`
limitations_found: OUTPUT parameter @ViolationCount (line 647) → return as second element of tuple; callers must unpack: `violations_df, count = audit_pricing_violations(...)`
OBJECT_ID check at line 656 → skip; no TODO needed, auto-managed

# TODO: Constraint — LEFT JOIN to AW19_PricingRules (lines 690-694) may match multiple rules per order line (no DISTINCT on RuleId in the join predicate); SQL Server would return one row per rule match. If multiple rules can match the same (CategoryName, TerritoryGroup, date) combination, add `.dropDuplicates(['SalesOrderDetailID'])` after the join or pre-filter rules to the most-specific match

usp_AW19_SalesCommissionPricingPipeline.dependency_signatures: def ufn_split_csv_int(csv_str: str | None) -> list[int]; class RunLogger: def __init__(self, spark, run_id, proc_name); def log_step(self, step_name, status, message=None, complete=False) -> None
conversion_instructions: Lines 135-143: Procedure parameters → `@dataclass class PipelineParams: start_date: date | None = None; end_date: date | None = None; territory_group_filter: str | None = None; customer_ids_csv: str | None = None; min_order_amt: Decimal = Decimal('0'); top_n_sales_person: int = 10; emit_debug: bool = False`
Line 145: `SET NOCOUNT ON` → skip
Line 147: `DECLARE @RunId UNIQUEIDENTIFIER = NEWID()` → `run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())`
Line 148: `DECLARE @ProcName SYSNAME = N'usp_AW19_SalesCommissionPricingPipeline'` → `PROC_NAME = 'usp_AW19_SalesCommissionPricingPipeline'`
Line 150: `EXEC dbo.usp_AW19_Log @RunId, @ProcName, N'START', N'START', ...` → `logger = RunLogger(spark, run_id, PROC_NAME); logger.log_step('START', 'START', 'Pipeline initiated')`

Lines 152-158: NULL date fallback SELECT MIN/MAX from SalesOrderHeader → `if params.start_date is None or params.end_date is None: bounds = spark.table('Sales.SalesOrderHeader').agg(F.min('OrderDate').alias('min_d'), F.max('OrderDate').alias('max_d')).first(); start_date = params.start_date or bounds['min_d']; end_date = params.end_date or bounds['max_d']`
Line 160: `BEGIN TRY` → `try:` block wrapping all step logic
Lines 163-166: STEP 1 — #CustomerFilter: `customer_ids = ufn_split_csv_int(params.customer_ids_csv)` — returns Python list[int]; skip DROP TABLE check
Line 168-170: `@HasCustFilter BIT = CASE WHEN EXISTS(...) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END` → `has_cust_filter = len(customer_ids) > 0`
Line 171: EXEC Log STEP1 → `logger.log_step('STEP1_CustomerFilter', 'OK')`

Lines 176-210: STEP 2 — #BaseOrders: Build multi-table DataFrame. INNER JOIN SalesTerritory on TerritoryID; LEFT JOINs to SalesPerson, Person.Person, HumanResources.Employee. For the sqa subquery (lines 200-205): pre-aggregate separately → `sqa = spark.table('Sales.SalesPersonQuotaHistory').where(F.col('QuotaDate').cast('date').between(start_date, end_date)).groupBy('BusinessEntityID').agg(F.sum('SalesQuota').alias('SalesQuota'))` then LEFT JOIN
Line 191: `COALESCE(sp.FirstName + ' ' + sp.LastName, 'Online/Unknown')` → `F.coalesce(F.concat_ws(' ', F.col('FirstName'), F.col('LastName')), F.lit('Online/Unknown'))`
Line 193: `COALESCE(sqa.SalesQuota, 0)` → `F.coalesce(F.col('SalesQuota'), F.lit(Decimal('0')))`
Lines 180-181: `CAST(h.OrderDate AS DATE)`, `CAST(h.ShipDate AS DATE)` → `F.col('OrderDate').cast('date')`, `F.col('ShipDate').cast('date')`
Line 206: `WHERE CAST(h.OrderDate AS DATE) BETWEEN @StartDate AND @EndDate` → `.where(F.col('OrderDate').cast('date').between(F.lit(start_date), F.lit(end_date)))`

Line 208: `@TerritoryGroupFilter IS NULL OR st.[Group] = @TerritoryGroupFilter` → apply conditionally: `if params.territory_group_filter: base = base.where(F.col('TerritoryGroup') == params.territory_group_filter)`
Lines 209-210: Customer filter: `if has_cust_filter: base = base.where(F.col('CustomerID').isin(customer_ids))`

Lines 212-214: Clustered + non-clustered indexes with INCLUDE → `.repartition('SalesOrderID').cache(); base_orders.count()  # force materialization` — add comment `# Indexes CX_BaseOrders / IX_BaseOrders_Cust / IX_BaseOrders_SP: no B-tree indexes in Spark; repartition simulates data locality`

Lines 221-257: STEP 3 — #Lines: JOIN SalesOrderDetail to #BaseOrders (base_orders_df), then to Production tables with LEFT JOINs. `p.ListPrice - p.StandardCost AS GrossMarginUnit` → `(F.col('ListPrice') - F.col('StandardCost')).alias('GrossMarginUnit')`. Cache after materialization.

Lines 264-309: STEP 4 — #CustomerTier: CTE `CustRevenue` → `cust_revenue = lines_df.groupBy('CustomerID', 'TerritoryGroup').agg(F.sum('LineTotal').alias('TotalRevenue'), F.sum(F.when(F.col('CategoryName')=='Bikes', F.col('LineTotal')).otherwise(0)).alias('BikeRevenue'), ..., F.countDistinct('SalesOrderID').alias('OrderCount'))`

Lines 286-307: 3-level nested CASE for CustomerTier → chained F.when: `F.when(F.col('TerritoryGroup') == 'North America', F.when(F.col('TotalRevenue') >= 50000, 'Gold').when(F.col('TotalRevenue') >= 20000, 'Silver').when(F.col('TotalRevenue') >= 5000, 'Bronze').otherwise('Standard')).when(F.col('TerritoryGroup') == 'Europe', F.when(...).when(...).otherwise('Standard')).when(F.col('TerritoryGroup') == 'Pacific', ...).otherwise(F.when(F.col('TotalRevenue') >= 50000, 'Gold')...)`

Lines 323-396: STEP 5 — #PricedLines: Three massive CASE blocks (A=DiscountPct, B=CommissionPct, C=TaxOverride). Convert each to F.when chain. Block A (lines 323-360) dispatches first on CategoryName+SubCategoryName, then TerritoryGroup, then CustomerTier — 3 nesting levels each generating DecimalType(5,4) literals. Block B (lines 362-386) dispatches on CategoryName→TerritoryGroup→CustomerTier. Block C (lines 388-395) is a simple 4-branch CASE on TerritoryGroup/CountryRegionCode. All string comparisons use exact match — verify CI gotcha #2 for TerritoryGroup/CustomerTier values
Lines 397-404: `INTO #PricedLines` + clustered/non-clustered indexes → `.cache(); priced_lines.count()` — add repartition on `('SalesOrderID', 'SalesOrderDetailID')` to simulate CX_PricedLines

Lines 411-450: STEP 6 — #LineFinals: `AdjustedRevenue = F.col('LineTotal') * (F.lit(1.0) - F.col('AppliedDiscountPct'))`. `NULLIF(pl.LineTotal * (1.0-...), 0)` at line 442 → `F.when(adj_rev_expr == 0, F.lit(None)).otherwise(adj_rev_expr)` — avoid re-computing adj_rev; assign to a named column first then reference it. `GrossMarginPct` at lines 439-443 divides by the same NULLIF expression — use `F.when(F.col('AdjustedRevenue') == 0, F.lit(None)).otherwise((F.col('AdjustedRevenue') - (F.col('StandardCost') * F.col('OrderQty'))) / F.col('AdjustedRevenue'))`

Lines 459-489: STEP 7 — #SPSummary: GROUP BY SalesPersonID, SalesPersonName, TerritoryGroup, TerritoryName. NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY SUM(lf.AdjustedRevenue) DESC) after GROUP BY → compute aggregation first, then apply window: `sp_summary = line_finals.where(F.col('SalesPersonID').isNotNull()).groupBy('SalesPersonID','SalesPersonName','TerritoryGroup','TerritoryName').agg(F.countDistinct('SalesOrderID').alias('TotalOrders'), F.sum('OriginalLineTotal').alias('TotalOriginalRevenue'), ...); ntile_window = Window.orderBy(F.col('TotalAdjustedRevenue').desc_nulls_last()); sp_summary = sp_summary.withColumn('RevenueQuartile', F.ntile(4).over(ntile_window))`

Lines 476-483: QuotaAttainmentBand CASE with NULLIF(MAX(bo.SalesQuota),0) denominator → `quota_col = F.coalesce(F.col('SalesQuota'), F.lit(Decimal('0'))); attainment = F.col('TotalAdjustedRevenue') / F.when(quota_col == 0, F.lit(None)).otherwise(quota_col); attainment_band = F.when(quota_col == 0, 'No Quota').when(attainment >= 1.20, 'Overachiever').when(attainment >= 1.00, 'On Target').when(attainment >= 0.80, 'Near Target').when(attainment >= 0.60, 'Below Target').otherwise('At Risk')`

Lines 496-520: STEP 8 — #CustomerSummary: CTE CustAgg → `cust_agg = line_finals_df.groupBy('CustomerID').agg(F.countDistinct('SalesOrderID').alias('OrderCount'), F.sum('AdjustedRevenue').alias('TotalRevenue'), ...)`. `DATEDIFF(DAY, MAX(lf.OrderDate), @EndDate)` at line 504 → **REVERSED ARGS**: `F.datediff(F.lit(end_date), F.col('LastOrderDate')).alias('RecencyDays')` — gotcha #1

Lines 515-517: Three NTILE(5) window functions: RScore, FScore, MScore → `recency_w = Window.orderBy(F.col('RecencyDays').asc_nulls_last()); freq_w = Window.orderBy(F.col('OrderCount').desc_nulls_last()); monetary_w = Window.orderBy(F.col('TotalRevenue').desc_nulls_last())` — all need explicit nulls direction — gotcha #3

Line 520: JOIN to Person.Person on BusinessEntityID = CustomerID → `.join(spark.table('Person.Person').select('BusinessEntityID','FirstName','LastName'), on=F.col('CustomerID') == F.col('BusinessEntityID'), how='inner')` then `F.concat_ws(' ', 'FirstName', 'LastName').alias('CustomerName')`

Lines 529-544: STEP 9 — #TerritoryBenchmarks: Simple GROUP BY on #SPSummary → `territory_benchmarks = sp_summary_df.groupBy('TerritoryGroup').agg(F.countDistinct('SalesPersonID').alias('SalesPersonCount'), F.sum('TotalAdjustedRevenue').alias('TerritoryTotalRevenue'), F.avg('TotalAdjustedRevenue').alias('TerritoryAvgRevSP'), ...)` — no window functions needed

Lines 551-592: STEP 10 — Final output with TOP N + correlated subqueries. First resolve correlated subqueries at lines 579-586: UniqueCustomers → pre-aggregate: `cust_per_sp = line_finals_df.join(customer_summary_df, on='CustomerID', how='inner').groupBy('SalesPersonID').agg(F.countDistinct('CustomerID').alias('UniqueCustomers'))`; AvgCustomerRFMScore → `rfm_per_sp = line_finals_df.join(customer_summary_df, on='CustomerID', how='inner').groupBy('SalesPersonID').agg(F.avg((F.col('RScore') + F.col('FScore') + F.col('MScore')).cast('double')).alias('AvgCustomerRFMScore'))`

Lines 570-576: NULLIF(tb.TerritoryAvgRevSP, 0) for percentage delta → `F.when(F.col('TerritoryAvgRevSP') == 0, F.lit(None)).otherwise((F.col('TotalAdjustedRevenue') - F.col('TerritoryAvgRevSP')) / F.col('TerritoryAvgRevSP')).alias('RevenueVsTerritoryPctDelta')`

Lines 587-589: `@StartDate AS SnapshotStartDate, @EndDate AS SnapshotEndDate, @RunId AS RunId` → `F.lit(start_date).alias('SnapshotStartDate'), F.lit(end_date).alias('SnapshotEndDate'), F.lit(run_id).alias('RunId')`
Lines 551-552: `SELECT TOP (@TopNSalesPerson) ... ORDER BY sp.TotalAdjustedRevenue DESC` → `.orderBy(F.col('TotalAdjustedRevenue').desc_nulls_last()).limit(params.top_n_sales_person)`
Lines 594-600: `END CATCH` → Python except: `except Exception as exc: err_msg = f'Error {type(exc).__name__} at pipeline: {str(exc)}'; logger.log_step('END', 'FAIL', err_msg, complete=True); raise`

Line 597: `CONCAT(N'Error ', ERROR_NUMBER(), N' at line ', ERROR_LINE(), N': ', ERROR_MESSAGE())` → Python f-string; no equivalent to ERROR_NUMBER()/ERROR_LINE() in Python — use `traceback.format_exc()` for full stack or `str(exc)` for message only
Line 549: Success log → `logger.log_step('END', 'SUCCESS', 'Completed', complete=True)` placed before final SELECT
gotchas_relevant: #1 DATEDIFF argument order reversed — line 504: `DATEDIFF(DAY, MAX(lf.OrderDate), @EndDate)` must become `F.datediff(F.lit(end_date), F.col('LastOrderDate'))` NOT `F.datediff(F.col('LastOrderDate'), F.lit(end_date))` — silent sign flip

#2 Case-sensitive string comparison — lines 208, 287, 292, 297, 324, 330, 336, 341, 347, 353, 364, 370, 375, 380, 390-393 all compare TerritoryGroup, CategoryName, SubCategoryName, CustomerTier against string literals; SQL Server CI_AS would match 'bikes'='Bikes'. PySpark will NOT. Verify source data always produces title-cased values; if not, add F.lower() normalization on both sides of each comparison

#3 NULL ordering in ORDER BY — line 484 NTILE ORDER BY TotalAdjustedRevenue DESC (NULLs last in SQL Server, FIRST in PySpark): use `.desc_nulls_last()`; lines 515-517 NTILE ORDER BY RecencyDays ASC / OrderCount DESC / TotalRevenue DESC: all need explicit nulls direction

#4 Window frame RANGE includes duplicates — lines 515-517 NTILE window functions do not have explicit frame — safe, NTILE does not use RANGE frame. However SUM window functions if added later must use `.rowsBetween()`

#5 Decimal precision loss — lines 432-448 compute `LineTotal * (1.0 - AppliedDiscountPct)`, `LineTotal * AppliedDiscountPct`, chained multiplications. Set `spark.sql.decimalOperations.allowPrecisionLoss=false` and ensure AppliedDiscountPct columns are cast to `DecimalType(5,4)` not left as double literals
limitations_found: Correlated scalar subqueries at lines 579-586 (UniqueCustomers, AvgCustomerRFMScore) — cannot auto-convert; must be pre-aggregated and flattened to LEFT JOINs before the final SELECT

Temp table indexes with INCLUDE columns at lines 213-214, 255-257, 403-404 — no B-tree indexes in Spark; use .repartition(col).cache() as best proxy; document as `# TODO: Constraint — INCLUDE columns on indexes not enforced; repartition provides data locality only`
OBJECT_ID tempdb checks at lines 163, 174, 219, 262, 316, 409, 457, 494, 527 — skip entirely; no TODO needed
